was an [infantry](/wiki/Infantry "Infantry") regiment of the [British Army](/wiki/British_Army "British Army"), raised in 1717 in [Annapolis Royal](/wiki/Annapolis_Royal "Annapolis Royal"), [Nova Scotia](/wiki/Nova_Scotia "Nova Scotia"). Under the [Childers Reforms](/wiki/Childers_Reforms "Childers Reforms") it amalgamated with the [82nd Regiment of Foot (Prince of Wales's Volunteers)](/wiki/82nd_Regiment_of_Foot_(Prince_of_Wales%27s_Volunteers) "82nd Regiment of Foot (Prince of Wales's Volunteers)") to form the [Prince of Wales's Volunteers (South Lancashire Regiment)](/wiki/South_Lancashire_Regiment "South Lancashire Regiment") in 1881.

The **40th (the 2nd Somersetshire) Regiment of Foot** was an [infantry](/wiki/Infantry "Infantry") regiment of the [British Army](/wiki/British_Army "British Army"), raised in 1717 in [Annapolis Royal](/wiki/Annapolis_Royal "Annapolis Royal"), [Nova Scotia](/wiki/Nova_Scotia "Nova Scotia"). Under the [Childers Reforms](/wiki/Childers_Reforms "Childers Reforms") it amalgamated with the [82nd Regiment of Foot (Prince of Wales's Volunteers)](/wiki/82nd_Regiment_of_Foot_(Prince_of_Wales%27s_Volunteers) "82nd Regiment of Foot (Prince of Wales's Volunteers)") to form the [Prince of Wales's Volunteers (South Lancashire Regiment)](/wiki/South_Lancashire_Regiment "South Lancashire Regiment") in 1881.

Governor of Nova Scotia [Richard Philipps](/wiki/Richard_Philipps "Richard Philipps"), First Commander of the 40th Regiment of Foot

### Formation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=40th_(the_2nd_Somersetshire)_Regiment_of_Foot&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Formation")]

The regiment was raised at [Annapolis Royal](/wiki/Annapolis_Royal "Annapolis Royal") in [Nova Scotia](/wiki/Nova_Scotia "Nova Scotia") by General [Richard Philipps](/wiki/Richard_Philipps "Richard Philipps") as the **Richard Philipps's Regiment of Foot** in August 1717 out of independent companies stationed in North America and the West Indies.[[1]](#cite_note-1)[[2]](#cite_note-2)

### Father Rale's War

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=40th_(the_2nd_Somersetshire)_Regiment_of_Foot&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Father Rale's War")]

Prior to [Father Rale's War](/wiki/Dummer%27s_War "Dummer's War"), the Mi'kmaq responded to the establishment of a British fort at [Canso, Nova Scotia](/wiki/Canso,_Nova_Scotia "Canso, Nova Scotia") by raiding the settlement's fishing station in 1720. Phillips sent a company of the 40th, under the command of Major [Lawrence Armstrong](/wiki/Lawrence_Armstrong "Lawrence Armstrong"), to take up garrison of a small fort in Canso built by a group of New England fishermen.[[3]](#cite_note-3) The Mi'kmaq continued preying on nearby shipping, forcing the garrison to take action in February 1723.[[4]](#cite_note-4) Serving as marines, the troops and local fishermen were able to disperse the attacking indigenous people. The next engagement came in July 1724 when a party of sixty Mi'kmaq attacked [Annapolis Royal](/wiki/Annapolis_Royal "Annapolis Royal"). The garrison responded with a poorly calculated sortie from the town's dilapidated fort, resulting in the death of a sergeant and private, the wounding of an officer and three privates, and the repulse of the troops. After some pillaging, the Mi'kmaq departed with a number of civilian prisoners.[[5]](#cite_note-militaryheritage.com-5)

From 1717 to 1743, Phillips' Regiment, garrisoning Annapolis, Placentia, and Canso, was successful in protecting settlers from Indian attacks, checking French influence in the area, and preserving the British foothold in Atlantic Canada.[[5]](#cite_note-militaryheritage.com-5)

### King George's War

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=40th_(the_2nd_Somersetshire)_Regiment_of_Foot&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: King George's War")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3f/WLA_lacma_Smibert_Scotland_portrait_of_Paul_Mascarene.jpg/250px-WLA_lacma_Smibert_Scotland_portrait_of_Paul_Mascarene.jpg)](/wiki/File:WLA_lacma_Smibert_Scotland_portrait_of_Paul_Mascarene.jpg)

Nova Scotia Lt. Gov. [Paul Mascarene](/wiki/Paul_Mascarene "Paul Mascarene"), commander of the 40th, portrait by [John Smybert](/wiki/John_Smybert "John Smybert"), 1729

At the outbreak of [King George's War](/wiki/King_George%27s_War "King George's War"), the French at Louisbourg immediately engaged in the [Raid on Canso](/wiki/Raid_on_Canso "Raid on Canso") in May 1744.[[6]](#cite_note-6) A flotilla containing 900 French regulars and militia. The four poorly supplied companies of Phillips' Regiment were forced to surrender. The town was destroyed and the prisoners sent to Louisbourg. Once the regiment's officers and men were paroled in September 1744, the regiment was evacuated to Boston where they provided valuable information on the defences of Louisbourg for the British [siege](/wiki/Siege_of_Louisbourg_(1745) "Siege of Louisbourg (1745)") the following year.[[5]](#cite_note-militaryheritage.com-5) Governor Shirley was having difficulty raising troops requested by Mascarene and therefore he ordered the ex-Canso garrison to Annapolis Royal.[[7]](#cite_note-7)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/ba/Private_man%2C_40th_1742.jpg/250px-Private_man%2C_40th_1742.jpg)](/wiki/File:Private_man,_40th_1742.jpg)

Private, 40th Regiment of Foot, Nova Scotia, 1742

The [Newfoundland Campaign](/wiki/Newfoundland_Campaign_(1744) "Newfoundland Campaign (1744)") started during August 1744. Captain Robert Young, of the 44-gun ship *Kinsale*, lying in St. John's, Newfoundland, received intelligence that five French ships were in the [port of Fishotte](/wiki/Fischot_Island "Fischot Island") and resolved on despatching an armed prize to attack them. The prize was named the *St. Philip*, and was manned by eighty men of the *Kinsale*'s crew, and commanded by one of her lieutenants,[[8]](#cite_note-8) and accompanied by three 10-gun colonial privateers. The *St. Philip* succeeded, after grounding several times, in reaching the *Moderate*, of twelve guns and seventy-five men, which was boarded and carried; then turning the *Moderate*'s guns against the remaining ships, without the assistance of the privateers (who did not get into the harbour in time), compelled the whole to surrender. The *St. Philip* had ten killed, and thirty wounded.[[9]](#cite_note-9) The loss on board the French ships was more severe. The five vessels, which had on board 18,000 quintals of fish and eighty tons of oil, mounted together sixty-six guns, and carried 342 men.[[10]](#cite_note-10)

In July 1744, three hundred Indians under command of a French priest named Le Loutre [attacked Annapolis](/wiki/Siege_of_Annapolis_Royal_(1744) "Siege of Annapolis Royal (1744)"), the only British garrison in Nova Scotia. Only eighty men of Phillips' Regiment were available to meet this threat, commanded by Lieutenant Colonel [Paul Mascarene](/wiki/Paul_Mascarene "Paul Mascarene"). Mascarene refused to surrender to Le Loutre. Le Loutre's party eventually burned a number of houses and withdrew. Following this, [George II](/wiki/George_II_of_Great_Britain "George II of Great Britain") authorized the reorganization of the regiment which increased to six regiments the garrison at Annapolis, with an authorized complement of 450. Initially only seventy additional men were received. Recruitment efforts continued and Governor Shirley sent 206 recruits in February 1746. Despite the additional manpower the regiment remained under strength. It was at this time that Captain [John Winslow](/wiki/John_Winslow_(British_Army_officer) "John Winslow (British Army officer)") first took command of a Philipp's regiment at Annapolis Royal, after being transferred from Newfoundland.[[11]](#cite_note-11) In September the enemy, this time three hundred regulars and militia with Indian support, reappeared outside the dilapidated earthworks of Annapolis Royal. After a four-week siege and lacking a train of artillery, the French withdrew from the defiant garrison.[[5]](#cite_note-militaryheritage.com-5)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a8/Thomas_McIlworth_-_General_John_Bradstreet_-_Google_Art_Project.jpg/250px-Thomas_McIlworth_-_General_John_Bradstreet_-_Google_Art_Project.jpg)](/wiki/File:Thomas_McIlworth_-_General_John_Bradstreet_-_Google_Art_Project.jpg)

[John Bradstreet](/wiki/John_Bradstreet "John Bradstreet") - member of the 40th, captured by the French in the [Raid on Canso](/wiki/Raid_on_Canso "Raid on Canso") in May 1744

A force of six hundred French and Indians again attempted to [take Annapolis in May 1745](/wiki/Siege_of_Annapolis_Royal_(1745) "Siege of Annapolis Royal (1745)"). This demonstration ended quickly with the French and Mi'kmaq being ordered back to help [defend Louisbourg from the British](/wiki/Siege_of_Louisbourg_(1745) "Siege of Louisbourg (1745)").[[12]](#cite_note-12)

The only other action seen by Phillips' Regiment occurred while serving as marines and seamen. A detachment from the garrison at St. John's, Newfoundland volunteered to serve on a captured twenty-gun ship for an expedition with three privateers to Fishotte Bay. The prize entered Fishotte Bay alone and engaged a number of anchored French ships. After five hours of fighting and the loss of ten killed and thirty wounded, the ship had captured three fourteen-gun and two twelve-gun enemy ships; forty six of their crews were killed and three hundred and thirty two made prisoner. The lagging privateers entered the harbour and assisted in the destruction of French fishing stages and the removal of enemy ships and prisoners.[[5]](#cite_note-militaryheritage.com-5)

By the end of the war Phillips' Regiment, after defending Britain's foothold in Nova Scotia with a skeleton complement, had its establishment raised to seventy men for each company. Men were quickly impressed in England for service in the regiment.[[5]](#cite_note-militaryheritage.com-5)

Between 1746 and 1748 the regiment contented itself with garrison duty at Annapolis and St. John's. With a continuing problem of finding recruits in Britain for the Philipp's regiment George II took the unusual step of allowing recruitment from the colonies for the British regiment.[[13]](#cite_note-13)

### Father Le Loutre's War

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=40th_(the_2nd_Somersetshire)_Regiment_of_Foot&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Father Le Loutre's War")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1e/Erasmus_James_Philipps%2C_Old_Burying_Ground%2C_Halifax%2C_Nova_Scotia.jpg/250px-Erasmus_James_Philipps%2C_Old_Burying_Ground%2C_Halifax%2C_Nova_Scotia.jpg)](/wiki/File:Erasmus_James_Philipps,_Old_Burying_Ground,_Halifax,_Nova_Scotia.jpg)

[Erasmus James Philipps](/wiki/Erasmus_James_Philipps "Erasmus James Philipps"), [Old Burying Ground (Halifax, Nova Scotia)](/wiki/Old_Burying_Ground_(Halifax,_Nova_Scotia) "Old Burying Ground (Halifax, Nova Scotia)")

The 40th was also actively engaged in [Father Le Loutre's War](/wiki/Father_Le_Loutre%27s_War "Father Le Loutre's War"). In July 1749, the grenadier company under Captain Handfield were sent to garrison the new settlement of Halifax founded the month earlier by the new Governor of Nova Scotia, [Edward Cornwallis](/wiki/Edward_Cornwallis "Edward Cornwallis"). A surprise attack by local Mi'kmaq in the [Siege of Grand Pré](/wiki/Siege_of_Grand_Pr%C3%A9 "Siege of Grand Pré") resulted in the capture of a detachment of the company including Lieutenant Hamilton and Handfield's son.[[14]](#cite_note-14) The captives were later returned to Halifax. Further engagements occurred with the Indians that year as the troops preserved the line of communication between Halifax and Annapolis Royal. Additional members of the regiment formed the garrison of [Fort Sackville](/wiki/Fort_Sackville_(Nova_Scotia) "Fort Sackville (Nova Scotia)") and established themselves at [Fort Edward](/wiki/Fort_Edward_(Nova_Scotia) "Fort Edward (Nova Scotia)").[[5]](#cite_note-militaryheritage.com-5)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/5/50/40thRegimentOfFootByDavid_Morier.png)](/wiki/File:40thRegimentOfFootByDavid_Morier.png)

40th Regiment of Foot by [David Morier](/wiki/David_Morier "David Morier"), 1751

By 1750 Cornwallis had taken over the colonelcy of the regiment. By the Royal Warrant of 1 July 1751, Cornwallis' Regiment was given the numerical distinction of the **40th Regiment of Foot**.[[15]](#cite_note-regiments-15)[[16]](#cite_note-16) Neglected for so many years by its former colonel, Cornwallis set about enhancing the condition of his new regiment. The companies in Newfoundland were rotated and discipline was improved. Desertion was poorly tolerated by Cornwallis. Of six deserters stationed at [Fort Vieux Logis](/wiki/Fort_Vieux_Logis "Fort Vieux Logis"), two were shot and the rest reprieved. Three other deserters were hanged and their bodies suspended in chains as a warning to others. Further changes happened in the 40th with Cornwallis' appointment of Major [Charles Lawrence](/wiki/Charles_Lawrence_(British_Army_officer) "Charles Lawrence (British Army officer)") of the 45th to the regiment's lieutenant colonelcy. Lawrence proved to be an energetic and effective military and administrative leader. After his appointment, Lawrence lead an expedition to the Missaguash River in August 1750 where he routed in the [Battle at Chignecto](/wiki/Battle_at_Chignecto "Battle at Chignecto") a superior number of Indians under Le Loutre. That fall he built [Fort Lawrence](/wiki/Fort_Lawrence "Fort Lawrence") across the river where the following spring the French would build Fort Beausejour.[[5]](#cite_note-militaryheritage.com-5)

In 1752 [Peregrine Hopson](/wiki/Peregrine_Hopson "Peregrine Hopson") succeeded Cornwallis as Nova Scotia's governor and colonel of the 40th Regiment. In poor health, Hopson returned to England in 1753 leaving the governing of the colony to Lawrence. In 1755 Hopson officially resigned as governor and Lawrence was appointed. However Hopson remained the colonel of the 40th Regiment until his death in 1759. (Afterward, for the first time since its formation, the regiment's colonel was not the governor of Nova Scotia. Hopson was succeeded as colonel in 1759 by John Barrington, followed by Robert Armiger in December 1760.)[[5]](#cite_note-militaryheritage.com-5)

### French and Indian War

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=40th_(the_2nd_Somersetshire)_Regiment_of_Foot&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: French and Indian War")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1f/MajorGeorgeScott.png/250px-MajorGeorgeScott.png)](/wiki/File:MajorGeorgeScott.png)

Captain [George Scott](/wiki/George_Scott_(British_Army_officer) "George Scott (British Army officer)") of the 40th by [John Singleton Copley](/wiki/John_Singleton_Copley "John Singleton Copley") (c.1758), [The Brook](/wiki/The_Brook_(New_York_City) "The Brook (New York City)")

By 1755 it was decided that the recently erected French [Fort Beausejour had to be removed](/wiki/Battle_of_Fort_Beaus%C3%A9jour "Battle of Fort Beauséjour") as a threat. The English force, including members of the 40th Regiment, was met by a large body of regulars and militia as they crossed the Missaquash river. This enemy force was attacked and dispersed quickly. Beausejour was subsequently laid siege to on 21 June and it surrendered four days later.[[17]](#cite_note-17)

Also in 1755, under the command of [John Handfield](/wiki/John_Handfield "John Handfield"), the 40th were engaged in the [Expulsion of the Acadians](/wiki/Expulsion_of_the_Acadians "Expulsion of the Acadians") from Annapolis Royal. After such a long stay in the colony, a number of the officers had married into the local Acadian population. Therefore, the deportation order forced officers to exile their own relations. Even the commander of the garrison, Major Handfield, had to deport his wife's "sister-in-law, nephews and nieces, uncles, aunts, and cousins." Handfield wrote to another officer performing the same task: "I heartily join with you in wishing that we were both of us got over this most disagreeable and troublesome part of the Service."[[5]](#cite_note-militaryheritage.com-5)

In 1757, after forty years in Annapolis Royal, the town where the regiment had been raised, 43rd relieved the 40th where it then went to Halifax to prepare for service in the Seven Years' War.[[18]](#cite_note-18) In 1758, under the command of Lieutenant-Colonel John Handfield, the regiment participated in the second siege of Louisbourg.[[19]](#cite_note-Brenda_Dunn_p._216-19)[[20]](#cite_note-20) Some companies of the regiment participated in the [Louisbourg Grenadiers](/wiki/Louisbourg_Grenadiers "Louisbourg Grenadiers") on its formation in 1759.[[21]](#cite_note-21) The following year, the regiment took part in the successful [three pronged attack against Montréal](/wiki/Montreal_Campaign "Montreal Campaign") in September which concluded the war there.[[22]](#cite_note-22)

The regiment moved to [Barbados](/wiki/Barbados "Barbados") in December 1761[[23]](#cite_note-23) and then took part in the [Battle of Havana](/wiki/Battle_of_Havana_(1762) "Battle of Havana (1762)") in June 1762.[[24]](#cite_note-24) In June 1763 the regiment rotated out of Havana to Annapolis Royal where it served until 1767 when it was transferred to [Ireland](/wiki/Ireland "Ireland").[[25]](#cite_note-25) This would be the first time in its 48-year history that the British regiment would serve on British soil.[[19]](#cite_note-Brenda_Dunn_p._216-19)

### American Revolution

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=40th_(the_2nd_Somersetshire)_Regiment_of_Foot&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: American Revolution")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cb/Edward_Lamson_Henry_-_The_Attack_on_Chew%27s_House_during_the_Battle_of_Germantown%2C_1777_-_1982.770_-_Art_Institute_of_Chicago.jpg/250px-Edward_Lamson_Henry_-_The_Attack_on_Chew%27s_House_during_the_Battle_of_Germantown%2C_1777_-_1982.770_-_Art_Institute_of_Chicago.jpg)](/wiki/File:Edward_Lamson_Henry_-_The_Attack_on_Chew%27s_House_during_the_Battle_of_Germantown,_1777_-_1982.770_-_Art_Institute_of_Chicago.jpg)

The Attack on Chew's House [held by the 40th Regiment] during the Battle of Germantown, 1777 (1878 painting by Edward Lamson Henry)

The regiment landed in [Boston](/wiki/Boston "Boston") for service in the [American Revolutionary War](/wiki/American_Revolutionary_War "American Revolutionary War") in June 1775.[[26]](#cite_note-26) It was evacuated from Boston in March 1776 and went to [Halifax](/wiki/Halifax,_Nova_Scotia "Halifax, Nova Scotia") from where a detachment was sent to [Georgia](/wiki/Georgia_(U.S._state) "Georgia (U.S. state)") to gather rice for the army in June 1776.[[27]](#cite_note-27) It saw action at the [Battle of Long Island](/wiki/Battle_of_Long_Island "Battle of Long Island") in August 1776,[[28]](#cite_note-28) the [Battle of Fort Washington](/wiki/Battle_of_Fort_Washington "Battle of Fort Washington") in November 1776[[29]](#cite_note-29) and the [Battle of Princeton](/wiki/Battle_of_Princeton "Battle of Princeton") in January 1777.[[30]](#cite_note-30) It was in combat again at the [Battle of Brandywine](/wiki/Battle_of_Brandywine "Battle of Brandywine") in September 1777[[31]](#cite_note-31) and the [Battle of Germantown](/wiki/Battle_of_Germantown "Battle of Germantown") in October 1777.[[32]](#cite_note-32) In November 1778 the regiment embarked for [Barbados](/wiki/Barbados "Barbados") and took part in the [Battle of St. Lucia](/wiki/Capture_of_St._Lucia "Capture of St. Lucia") in December 1778.[[33]](#cite_note-33) The regiment was then based in [Antigua](/wiki/Antigua "Antigua") until June 1781 when it returned to [Staten Island](/wiki/Staten_Island "Staten Island")[[34]](#cite_note-34) and then took part in the [Battle of Groton Heights](/wiki/Battle_of_Groton_Heights "Battle of Groton Heights") in September 1781:[[35]](#cite_note-35) Major William Montgomery, commanding the regiment was killed in the assault.[[36]](#cite_note-36) In August 1782, the regiment took a county title as the **40th (the 2nd Somersetshire) Regiment of Foot**.[[15]](#cite_note-regiments-15) The regiment embarked for home in November 1783.[[37]](#cite_note-37)

### Napoleonic Wars

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=40th_(the_2nd_Somersetshire)_Regiment_of_Foot&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Napoleonic Wars")]

In January 1794 the regiment embarked for [Barbados](/wiki/Barbados "Barbados") for service in the [French Revolutionary Wars](/wiki/French_Revolutionary_Wars "French Revolutionary Wars") and took part in the capture of [Martinique](/wiki/Martinique "Martinique") in March 1794[[38]](#cite_note-38) and the attack on [Guadeloupe](/wiki/Guadeloupe "Guadeloupe") in April 1794: some members of the regiment became [prisoners of war](/wiki/Prisoner_of_war "Prisoner of war") and were held on the island for over a year.[[39]](#cite_note-39) The rest of the regiment returned home and in June 1794 embarked for [Ostend](/wiki/Ostend "Ostend"):[[40]](#cite_note-40) the regiment was not significantly engaged and returned home again in April 1795.[[41]](#cite_note-41) The regiment returned to the [West Indies](/wiki/West_Indies "West Indies") in summer 1795 and took part in an attack on the French troops on [Saint Vincent](/wiki/Saint_Vincent_(Antilles) "Saint Vincent (Antilles)") in September 1795.[[42]](#cite_note-42) It moved to [Saint-Domingue](/wiki/Saint-Domingue "Saint-Domingue") in July 1797 before returning home in December 1798.[[43]](#cite_note-43) The regiment also took part in the [Anglo-Russian invasion of Holland](/wiki/Anglo-Russian_invasion_of_Holland "Anglo-Russian invasion of Holland") in August 1799[[44]](#cite_note-44) and fought at the [Battle of Bergen](/wiki/Battle_of_Bergen_(1799) "Battle of Bergen (1799)") in September 1799[[45]](#cite_note-45) and the [Battle of Alkmaar](/wiki/Battle_of_Alkmaar_(1799) "Battle of Alkmaar (1799)") in October 1799[[46]](#cite_note-46) before returning home in November 1799.[[47]](#cite_note-47)

The regiment also took part in the expedition to the [Mediterranean](/wiki/Mediterranean_Sea "Mediterranean Sea") and fought at the [Battle of Abukir](/wiki/Battle_of_Abukir_(1801) "Battle of Abukir (1801)")[[48]](#cite_note-48) and [Battle of Alexandria](/wiki/Battle_of_Alexandria_(1801) "Battle of Alexandria (1801)") in March 1801[[49]](#cite_note-49) and then returned to England in October 1801.[[50]](#cite_note-50) The regiment was part of the force assembled for the [invasions of the River Plate](/wiki/British_invasions_of_the_River_Plate "British invasions of the River Plate") in September 1806[[51]](#cite_note-51) and took part in the attack on Battle of Montevideo in February 1807[[52]](#cite_note-52) before returning to England in December 1807.[[53]](#cite_note-53)

In July 1808 the regiment embarked for [Portugal](/wiki/Portugal "Portugal"), as part of [Sir Arthur Wellesley](/wiki/Arthur_Wellesley,_1st_Duke_of_Wellington "Arthur Wellesley, 1st Duke of Wellington")'s army, for service in the [Peninsular War](/wiki/Peninsular_War "Peninsular War").[[54]](#cite_note-54) It fought at the [Battle of Roliça](/wiki/Battle_of_Roli%C3%A7a "Battle of Roliça") in August 1808, the [Battle of Vimeiro](/wiki/Battle_of_Vimeiro "Battle of Vimeiro") later that month[[55]](#cite_note-55) and the [Battle of Talavera](/wiki/Battle_of_Talavera "Battle of Talavera") in July 1809.[[56]](#cite_note-56) The regiment also took part in the [Battle of Bussaco](/wiki/Battle_of_Bussaco "Battle of Bussaco") in September 1810 and then fell back to the [Lines of Torres Vedras](/wiki/Lines_of_Torres_Vedras "Lines of Torres Vedras") in October 1810.[[57]](#cite_note-57) The regiment later took part in the [Siege of Ciudad Rodrigo](/wiki/Siege_of_Ciudad_Rodrigo_(1812) "Siege of Ciudad Rodrigo (1812)") in January 1812,[[58]](#cite_note-58) the [Siege of Badajoz](/wiki/Siege_of_Badajoz_(1812) "Siege of Badajoz (1812)") in March 1812[[59]](#cite_note-59) and the [Battle of Salamanca](/wiki/Battle_of_Salamanca "Battle of Salamanca") in July 1812[[60]](#cite_note-60) as well as the [Battle of Vitoria](/wiki/Battle_of_Vitoria "Battle of Vitoria") in June 1813.[[61]](#cite_note-61) It then pursued the French Army into France and fought at the [Battle of the Pyrenees](/wiki/Battle_of_the_Pyrenees "Battle of the Pyrenees") in July 1813,[[62]](#cite_note-62) the [Battle of Nivelle](/wiki/Battle_of_Nivelle "Battle of Nivelle") in November 1813[[63]](#cite_note-63) and the [Battle of Orthez](/wiki/Battle_of_Orthez "Battle of Orthez") in February 1814[[64]](#cite_note-64) before also taking part [Battle of Toulouse](/wiki/Battle_of_Toulouse_(1814) "Battle of Toulouse (1814)") in April 1814.[[65]](#cite_note-65) The regiment returned home in June 1814.[[66]](#cite_note-66) In October 1814 the regiment was sent to [New Orleans](/wiki/New_Orleans "New Orleans") for service in the [War of 1812](/wiki/War_of_1812 "War of 1812") but recalled upon the ending of that conflict in March 1815.[[67]](#cite_note-67)

In May 1815 the regiment was rushed to join with Wellington's army just before the [Battle of Waterloo](/wiki/Battle_of_Waterloo "Battle of Waterloo") commenced.[[68]](#cite_note-68) Initially placed in reserve, they were later in the day moved to the centre of his line to a position near [La Haye Sainte](/wiki/La_Haye_Sainte "La Haye Sainte").[[69]](#cite_note-69) They held firm all day and helped drive off [Napoleon](/wiki/Napoleon "Napoleon")'s final massed infantry attack, ultimately losing 170 killed or wounded, including their commanding officer Major Arthur Rowley Heyland.[[70]](#cite_note-70) The regiment then formed part of the Army of Occupation until returning to England in April 1817.[[71]](#cite_note-71)

### The Victorian era

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=40th_(the_2nd_Somersetshire)_Regiment_of_Foot&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: The Victorian era")]

In 1823 the regiment was dispatched in small detachments in convict ships to [New South Wales](/wiki/New_South_Wales "New South Wales")[[72]](#cite_note-72) where it served at both [Sydney](/wiki/Sydney "Sydney"), and [Van Diemen's Land](/wiki/Van_Diemen%27s_Land "Van Diemen's Land"), where they participated in the [Black War](/wiki/Black_War "Black War").[[73]](#cite_note-Odgers17-73) It was then transferred to [Bombay](/wiki/Mumbai "Mumbai"), with the first units of the 40th leaving Australia in 1828.[[74]](#cite_note-74) While in India, the regiment was stationed in [Belgaum](/wiki/Belgaum "Belgaum")[[75]](#cite_note-75) and then [Pune](/wiki/Pune "Pune")[[76]](#cite_note-76) before moving to [Bombay](/wiki/Mumbai "Mumbai").[[77]](#cite_note-77) In January 1839 the regiment was sent to the [Sindh](/wiki/Sindh "Sindh")[[78]](#cite_note-78) and took part in the capture of [Karachi](/wiki/Karachi "Karachi").[[79]](#cite_note-79) The regiment camped at [Quetta](/wiki/Quetta "Quetta") on their way to [Afghanistan](/wiki/Afghanistan "Afghanistan") where they experienced one of the worst out breaks of disease of any regiment of the British Army.[[80]](#cite_note-80) It arrived in [Kandahar](/wiki/Kandahar "Kandahar") in October 1841[[81]](#cite_note-81) and then fought under General [William Nott](/wiki/William_Nott_(British_general) "William Nott (British general)") at the [Battle of Kabul](/wiki/Battle_of_Kabul_(1842) "Battle of Kabul (1842)") in August 1842 during the [First Anglo-Afghan War](/wiki/First_Anglo-Afghan_War "First Anglo-Afghan War").[[82]](#cite_note-82) The regiment returned to India in December 1842[[83]](#cite_note-83) and fought at the Battle of Maharajpore in December 1843 during the [Gwalior campaign](/wiki/Gwalior_campaign "Gwalior campaign").[[84]](#cite_note-84) It returned to England in September 1845.[[85]](#cite_note-85)

The regiment returned to Australia in June 1852[[86]](#cite_note-86) and served in [Victoria](/wiki/Victoria_(Australia) "Victoria (Australia)") where it suppressed the [Eureka Rebellion](/wiki/Eureka_Rebellion "Eureka Rebellion") in December 1854.[[73]](#cite_note-Odgers17-73) It also took part in the [Taranaki](/wiki/First_Taranaki_War "First Taranaki War") and the [Waikato](/wiki/Invasion_of_the_Waikato "Invasion of the Waikato") campaigns in the early 1860s during the [New Zealand Wars](/wiki/New_Zealand_Wars "New Zealand Wars").[[87]](#cite_note-87) The regiment arrived home in August 1866[[88]](#cite_note-88) but returned to India in September 1872.[[89]](#cite_note-89)

As part of the [Cardwell Reforms](/wiki/Cardwell_Reforms "Cardwell Reforms") of the 1870s, where single-battalion regiments were linked together to share a single depot and recruiting district in the United Kingdom, the 40th was linked with the [82nd Regiment of Foot (Prince of Wales's Volunteers)](/wiki/82nd_Regiment_of_Foot_(Prince_of_Wales%27s_Volunteers) "82nd Regiment of Foot (Prince of Wales's Volunteers)"), and assigned to district no. 14 at [Peninsula Barracks, Warrington](/wiki/Peninsula_Barracks,_Warrington "Peninsula Barracks, Warrington").[[90]](#cite_note-training-90) On 1 July 1881 the [Childers Reforms](/wiki/Childers_Reforms "Childers Reforms") came into effect and the regiment amalgamated with the 82nd Regiment of Foot (Prince of Wales's Volunteers) to form the [Prince of Wales's Volunteers (South Lancashire Regiment)](/wiki/South_Lancashire_Regiment "South Lancashire Regiment").[[15]](#cite_note-regiments-15)

## Battle honours

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=40th_(the_2nd_Somersetshire)_Regiment_of_Foot&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Battle honours")]

The battle honours of the regiment were as follows:[[15]](#cite_note-regiments-15)

* Egypt, [Montevideo](/wiki/British_invasions_of_the_River_Plate "British invasions of the River Plate"), [Roliça](/wiki/Battle_of_Roli%C3%A7a "Battle of Roliça"), [Vimiero](/wiki/Battle_of_Vimeiro "Battle of Vimeiro"), [Talavera](/wiki/Battle_of_Talavera "Battle of Talavera"), [Badajoz](/wiki/Siege_of_Badajoz_(1812) "Siege of Badajoz (1812)"), [Salamanca](/wiki/Battle_of_Salamanca "Battle of Salamanca"), [Vittoria](/wiki/Battle_of_Vitoria "Battle of Vitoria"), [Pyrenees](/wiki/Battle_of_the_Pyrenees "Battle of the Pyrenees"), [Nivelle](/wiki/Battle_of_Nivelle "Battle of Nivelle"), [Orthes](/wiki/Battle_of_Orthez "Battle of Orthez"), Peninsula, [Toulouse](/wiki/Battle_of_Toulouse_(1814) "Battle of Toulouse (1814)"), [Waterloo](/wiki/Battle_of_Waterloo "Battle of Waterloo"), [Candahar](/wiki/Kandahar "Kandahar") (1842), [Ghuznee](/wiki/Ghazni "Ghazni") (1842), [Cabool](/wiki/Battle_of_Kabul_(1842) "Battle of Kabul (1842)") (1842), [Maharajpore](/wiki/Gwalior_campaign "Gwalior campaign"), [New Zealand](/wiki/New_Zealand_Wars "New Zealand Wars")
